---
title: "Multilevel & Hierarchical Models"
subtitle: "Partial pooling, varying effects, and nested data"
---

## Overview

Data in economics and the health sciences are rarely flat. Patients sit within vårdcentraler, vårdcentraler within regioner; pupils within schools; repeated measurements within individuals. Ignoring this nesting --- by either pooling everything together or analysing each group in isolation --- throws away information and produces standard errors that are simply wrong.

Multilevel (hierarchical, or mixed) models take the structure seriously. Their defining move is **partial pooling**: each group's estimate is pulled toward the overall average by an amount that depends on how much data the group has and how much groups genuinely differ. Small, noisy groups borrow strength from the whole; large groups stand on their own. The same machinery lets intercepts *and* slopes vary across groups, decomposes variation into within- and between-group components, and models individual trajectories over time.

This module is the natural extension of random effects: it generalises the single random intercept into a flexible framework for varying effects, variance components, and growth.

## Pages in This Module

- [Partial Pooling and Shrinkage](partial-pooling-shrinkage.qmd) --- Borrowing strength across groups.
- [Random Intercepts and Random Slopes](random-intercepts-slopes.qmd) --- Letting relationships vary across groups.
- [Variance Components and the ICC](variance-components-icc.qmd) --- How much variation lives at each level.
- [Growth Curve Models](growth-models.qmd) --- Multilevel models for change over time.
